data.title: 'It's Expensive for a Reason,' Says Top Investor About Palantir Stock
data.description:
Palantir (NASDAQ:PLTR) stock appears to have lost some momentum after its remarkable run. Shares have mostly drifted lower since the company's blowout Q3 earnings in early November, raising the possibility that the AI darling's extremely elevated valuation is finally catching up with it.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

The PLTR bears have been predicting a decline for some time now, arguing that there's simply no justification for the company's share price to enjoy a valuation multiple that's so far ahead of its peers. After treading water for the last three months, PLTR's price-to-earnings ratio remains around 400x.

Top investor James Foord understands why those concerns are gaining traction, but he doesn't believe they tell the whole story.

"PLTR looks expensive only if you misjudge its market," declares the 5-star investor, who is among the top 2% of stock pros covered by TipRanks.

Foord identifies three phases of the AI revolution: compute, infrastructure, and software. He claims that value has been moving up the chain, starting with chips and GPUs and then shifting to data centers and their supporting features. Now, he believes the time has come for software to reign supreme, and Palantir is one of the best positioned to ride the next iteration of the AI wave.

"AI has matured, and as such, it's time to invest in the companies that are actually creating profit for it, not just spending billions laying down the infrastructure," states Foord.

On that note, the investor claims that Palantir has one of the largest TAMs around, with defense spending ballooning and the enterprise software market expected to reach $500 billion by 2030.

That makes Palantir "uniquely positioned" to enjoy the spoils up ahead. With its first-mover advantage and subsequent high costs of switching, Palantir could even replicate the success of the first operating systems.

"Palantir is a clear winner, priced as such, but in my opinion, still very much worth holding for the long term as growth and earnings compound," concludes Foord.

The investor is putting his money where his mouth is, rating PLTR a Strong Buy. (To watch Foord's track record, click here)

Wall Street isn't quite as ready to stake such a strong claim. With 5 Buys, 10 Holds, and 2 Sells, PLTR carries a consensus Hold (i.e., Neutral) rating. Its 12-month average price target of $192.88 points to an upside of ~13% from current levels. (See PLTR stock analysis)
data.author: Gabe Ross

data.title: U.S. Stock Futures Drop as Trade Tensions Hit Markets After Holiday
data.description:
U.S. stock futures fell late Monday after investors reacted to President Donald Trump's comments on Greenland and new tariff threats. Futures on the Nasdaq 100 (NDX), the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA), and the S&P 500 (SPX) Index were down 1.05%, 0.84%, and 0.94%, respectively, at 8:45 p.m. EDT on January 19.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

In a Truth Social post on Saturday, Trump said the U.S. would impose an additional 10% tariff on eight countries, including the United Kingdom, starting on February 1. Afterward, the rate will rise to 25% on June 1. The tariffs will remain in place until the U.S. secures a deal to acquire Greenland.

Monday night marked the first full chance for U.S. investors to react to the trade news, as markets were closed earlier in the day for the Martin Luther King Jr. holiday.

Last week, the S&P 500 fell 0.4%, while the Dow Jones Industrial Average lost 0.3%. The Nasdaq Composite dropped 0.7% for the week. This came despite a brief lift in chip stocks tied to artificial intelligence after Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) posted strong fourth-quarter results on Thursday.

Earnings will also be in focus this week, with several companies reporting results, including Netflix (NFLX), 3M (MMM), United Airlines (UAL), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), Charles Schwab (SCHW), and Intel (INTC).

data.author: Shalu Saraf

data.title: Quantum Computing News: Investors Price In Quantum Threats as Post-Quantum Chips Scale Up
data.description:
Welcome to Monday's update on quantum computing. As the year moves forward, attention is shifting from research milestones toward security planning, capital markets activity, and early commercial tools that already affect investors. As always, this update focuses on practical developments tied to quantum systems and the firms building around them.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

In today's edition, we cover new progress in quantum simulation on classical hardware, rising concern around quantum-driven security risk, and continued movement across public and pre-public quantum companies.

Let's begin.

Classical Hardware Advances Support Quantum Research

First, MicroCloud Hologram Inc. (HOLO) is showing how classical hardware can still play a key role in quantum research. The company announced progress using field programmable gate arrays to speed up tensor network simulations used in quantum physics and spin models.

By moving key math tasks directly into custom hardware circuits, the system reduced memory strain and improved parallel work. Testing showed speeds about 70% faster than standard CPUs and more than double the energy efficiency. While this is not quantum hardware, it supports research teams that model quantum systems long before full-scale machines arrive.

As a result, tools like this may help shorten research cycles and lower costs for labs and firms working on quantum design.

Quantum Security Moves from Theory to Portfolio Risk

Next, quantum security is no longer just a technical topic. Jefferies recently removed Bitcoin (BTC) from a key Asia-focused portfolio due to long-term quantum risk. The firm cited the chance that future quantum systems could weaken the cryptography that protects digital assets.

While the timeline remains unclear, the concern is about the risk that cannot be reversed once it appears. This decision shows that large investors are now weighing quantum threats when shaping long-term asset exposure. At the same time, post-quantum encryption work continues as firms look to protect data created today from future attacks.

This shift places security planning alongside price and policy in portfolio reviews.

Post Quantum Chips Gain Real World Scale

Meanwhile, SEALSQ Corp. (LAES) is expanding its role in post quantum security through hardware. The company is already deploying secure chips that use quantum-safe cryptography in millions of devices worldwide.

These chips act as hardware roots of trust and aim to protect data against future decryption risks. Management has stressed that the threat is present now because data can be collected today and decoded later. As a result, firms are being urged to adopt protection well ahead of quantum breakthroughs.

This approach reflects a longer view of risk that matters for governments and large enterprises.

Leadership and Listings Signal Market Maturity

Several quantum firms are preparing for the next stage of growth. SEEQC Inc. announced plans to go public through a merger that values the firm at about $1 billion. The company builds control and readout chips that sit close to quantum processors and help reduce system delays.

In addition, firms like Quantinuum, IonQ (IONQ), Xanadu, PsiQuantum, and Infleqtion continue to add leaders with experience in defense, finance, security, and public markets. These moves suggest the industry is shifting from lab focus toward scale, regulation, and investor readiness.

We used TipRanks' Comparison Tool to line up all the stocks appearing in the piece alongside quantum computing stocks. It's a quick way to see how they stack up and where the field could be heading.
data.author: Ran Melamed

data.title: How the Greenland Debacle Creates a Bullish Tailwind for Coinbase Stock (COIN)
data.description:
Just when you thought that the cryptocurrency sector was rising from its prior malaise, a sharp downturn brought everyone back to reality — and this appears to have negative implications for crypto wallet and exchange provider Coinbase Global (COIN). Because the company is intricately tied to the fortunes of the blockchain, the sudden loss of momentum has naturally sparked concern for COIN stock.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

And just what was this impact? Recently, President Donald Trump threatened to impose punitive tariffs on multiple European countries over a dispute involving Greenland, the vast Arctic territory that is part of the Kingdom of Denmark. According to the most recent reports, no less than eight countries are in the crossfire and can expect to face a 10% tariff on exported goods to the U.S. beginning Feb. 1, and will then rise to 25% on June 1.
data.author: Joshua Enomoto

data.title: AMD Files Insider Stock Sale Notice Ahead of Q4 Earnings. What Investors Should Know
data.description:
Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) filed a Form 144 with the SEC, which indicates that an insider may sell shares in the near future. Such filings are commonly used for planned or routine transactions and do not indicate a change in the company's outlook.

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions

The filing revealed that AMD executive Ava Hahn submitted the notice on January 16, outlining plans to sell up to 2,442 shares worth about $572,000. Under Rule 144, insiders must file in advance before selling controlled shares, a step often tied to personal financial planning.

Insider Sentiment for AMD Stock Is Bearish

Based on other informative transactions from the last three months, corporate insiders have sold $2.2 million worth of AMD stock. Overall, TipRanks' Insider Trading Activity Tool shows that insider confidence in the stock is currently Very Negative.
data.author: Shalu Saraf

data.title: QBTS Is Surging. Does It Beat IONQ, RGTI, and QUBT for 2026?
data.description:
IonQ (IONQ), Rigetti Computing (RGTI), Quantum Computing inc.(QUBT), and D-Wave Quantum (QBTS) are four pure play names, but the market is treating them very differently. On the tape, D-Wave Quantum is the clear short-term winner. The stock is up 358.35% over the past year, far ahead of Rigetti (83.26%), IonQ (12.12%), and Quantum Computing (10.53%).

Additionally, on the analyst side, IonQ and D-Wave Quantum both show Strong Buy, while Rigetti and Quantum Computing show Moderate Buy. The average price targets point to large upside across the group: $75.91 for IonQ, $39.78 for Rigetti, $18.00 for Quantum Computing, and $40.23 for D-Wave Quantum.

Even though all four sit in "quantum," their tech paths are not the same, and that shapes how to think about 2026 versus the long run. In this comparison piece, we'll examine how all four stocks are set for 2026 and beyond,

IONQ

First, IonQ uses trapped-ion technology and sells access through major cloud hubs. TipRanks' AI Analyst highlights strong revenue growth, key buys that expand reach, and a strong cash position. At the same time, it flags big losses, high spending, and weak cash flow. For 2026, the near-term story is execution. The next earnings date shown is April 1, 2026, with a sales forecast of $40.38 million and an EPS forecast of -$0.52. The stock also looks steadier than the others on the chart, but it still trades like a high-risk growth name.

data.title: This Top Analyst Says Alibaba (BABA) Is a 'Solid AI Proxy.' Here's Why
data.description:
Top DBS analyst Andy Yu remains bullish on Alibaba (BABA), calling the stock a "solid AI proxy" as the company pushes deeper into AI-driven execution rather than simple chat tools. The firm reiterated its BUY rating on the stock, even after trimming its price target to $215 from $225, as confidence in Alibaba's long-term strategy remains intact.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Alibaba's Qwen Expands Beyond Simple Chat

Yu highlighted Alibaba's launch of Qwen Task Assistant 1.0 as a meaningful step forward in AI use. Unlike standard chatbots, Qwen is built to carry out tasks end-to-end — from planning to payment — across shopping, travel, food delivery, and daily services.

As a result, AI becomes a direct driver of transactions, not just engagement. This strengthens Alibaba's ecosystem by keeping users within its apps and improving conversion across core services.

Why This Supports the Bull Case

DBS said that Alibaba's AI rollout strengthens both its core business and emerging growth areas:

  • Qwen acts as a traffic gateway, pulling users directly into Alibaba's core services such as e-commerce, payments, travel, and local services. This reinforces the company's long-term super-app strategy.
  • Deep integration creates a competitive edge. Users can move from search to payment in one flow, reducing friction and limiting the need to leave Alibaba's ecosystem.
  • The biggest upside is in e-commerce and online travel. These are high-intent categories where AI can shorten research time, improve decisions, and lift conversion rates.  This directly supports market share defense and CMR stability, which the analyst views as a key driver for the stock.

Additional Growth Drivers Support the Outlook

Beyond AI-led improvements to core commerce, DBS also pointed to growing contributions from cloud and quick commerce:

  • Cloud momentum is building on rising AI demand. Management has hinted at lifting its three-year cloud revenue target of RMB380 billion, as AI workloads exceed expectations. DBS forecasts Alibaba Cloud revenue to grow at a 31% CAGR between FY25 and FY28.
  • Quick commerce continues to show progress. Alibaba's food-delivery market share has reached around 40%, while user retention has held up better than expected despite lower subsidies. DBS also sees growing synergy with the core e-commerce platform and estimates quick commerce could drive up to RMB1 trillion in incremental GMV over time.

Is Alibaba Stock a Buy Now?  

Wall Street remains constructive on the stock. Alibaba stock carries a Strong Buy consensus rating, based on 13 Buy ratings and one Hold over the past three months. The average BABA price target stands at $203.66, suggesting roughly 23.13% upside from current levels.
data.author: Shalu Saraf

data.title: Trump's New Tariffs Could Send the UK Spiraling into a Recession
data.description:
President Trump will impose an additional 10% tariff on eight countries, including the United Kingdom, starting on February 1. Afterward, the rate will rise to 25% on June 1. The tariffs will remain in place until the U.S. secures a deal to acquire Greenland.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

That could send the UK into a recession, according to Capital Economics. The firm estimates that a 25% tariff on top of the country's existing 10% tariff will decrease its gross domestic product (GDP) by 0.75%, equivalent to a $25.15 billion hit. "With the UK economy currently growing by 0.2-0.3pc a quarter, if this hit came all at once it could trigger a recession," said Capital Economics Chief UK Economist Paul Dales.

Starmer Calls for 'Calm Discussion' on Greenland Dispute

Unlike several other European leaders, UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer has talked down the prospect of retaliatory tariffs and instead proposed a negotiation process. "I think that this can be resolved, and should be resolved, through calm discussion," he said. Starmer added that imposing tariffs is the wrong approach when addressing differences within an alliance and that the people of Greenland should have control over their future.

European Union leaders are scheduled to meet this Thursday for an emergency summit to discuss their options, which include reviving a $103 billion tariff package on U.S. goods that was shelved last year after the two sides reached a trade agreement.

data.title: These European Stocks Could Be in the Firing Line in Trump's Greenland Tariff War
data.description:
A new U.S.-EU trade war is brewing and could leave several European stocks across pharmaceutical, auto, and fashion possibly exposed.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

What's Happening?

Over the weekend, President Donald Trump, threatened a 10% tariff — starting on February 1 and expanded to 25% by June 1 — on eight European allies to push forward his ambitions to make Greenland part of the U.S. The American leader noted that he plans to maintain the higher tariff until an acquisition deal is reached.

Already, the U.S. trade deal agreed with the bloc in July last year imposed a blanket 15% tariff on most EU exports, plus a steeper 50% tariff on European steel and aluminium. The latest tariff could pile on this, causing great inconvenience to European businesses across sectors, from pharmaceutical to auto and luxury.

Which Pharma Stocks Could be Exposed?

The EU pharmaceutical sector — which accounts for the bloc's biggest export to the U.S. — could face some of the strongest headwinds from this trend. With branded medicines already taxed at 15%, additional taxes could be a burden for companies such as Danish drugmaker Novo Nordisk (NVO), Switzerland-based Roche (RHHBY) and Novartis (NVS), and France's Sanofi (SNY), among others.

According to U.S. Pharmacopeia, in 2024, only 15% of the active ingredients in branded drugs sold in the U.S. were made domestically — 43% of them came from the EU. While the Trump administration continues to pressure pharma companies to expand domestic production, it would likely take time to see the fruits of such an effort.

Which Auto Stocks Could Face Tariff Headwinds?

Next is the auto sector, even as machinery and vehicles remain the second-largest export sector for the EU despite shrinking trade surplus between the bloc and the U.S. This brings into focus European automobile manufacturers such as Mercedes-Benz Group (MBGAF), Volkswagen (VWAGY), BMW (BMW), and Stellantis (STLA).

According to the European Automobile Manufacturers' Association, the U.S. remains the second largest market for new EU vehicle exports after the UK. However, domestic production across U.S. plants could help reduce impact.

Which Fashion Stocks Could Be Hit?

In the fashion space, companies such as Italian fashion powerhouse LVMH (LVMUY), the UK's Burberry (BURBY), and Italy-based Brunello Cucinelli (BC), could face some exposure as U.S. is a key fashion market for their brands.

What Are the Best Stocks to Buy?

data.author: Solomon Oladipupo
